The Story

A kid from Oakland
who refused to stay small.

"I remember coming to MSU as a freshman from a small town, and this was such a big campus. I felt like I was lost so many times. I decided I could make an app to help people."

Terence Williams was born and raised in Oakland, Mississippi — a small town of fewer than 600 people, where the path to Silicon Valley isn't paved, it's carved. He attended grade school in neighboring Coffeeville, where the Upward Bound TRiO Program first exposed him to engineering. He enrolled at Mississippi State University as an electrical engineering major, walked into a First Year Experience class that introduced him to iOS development, and never looked back.

By the time he graduated, he had published 12 mobile apps to the App Store, won a 2016 Apple WWDC Scholarship (one of 350 selected worldwide), received a White House Frontier nomination from President Obama, and founded Nobility Tech. The app he built to help freshmen navigate campus — Bully Walk — provided over 1.3 million routes and has since evolved into GradWay, now live at 15+ universities.

After graduating, Terence climbed the professional ranks of software engineering, ultimately reaching the peak of his corporate career as a Software Engineering Manager. Then in 2024, something rare happened: Moxy Messenger, the scheduling app he had been building on the side, gave him the freedom to step back — and he spent a year living and exploring Thailand. Not retired. Not on vacation. Building, reflecting, and returning with a new level of clarity about what he wants to create next.

Now Terence is in his consulting era — bringing a decade of hard-won experience and the perspective of someone who has built at every level, from undergrad bedroom to corporate boardroom to a beach in Southeast Asia.

The Moxy Chapter

He built an app.
The app bought him
a year in Thailand.

After years of climbing the corporate ladder, ultimately reaching the level of Software Engineering Manager, Terence had proven everything he needed to prove in the traditional tech career path. He had shipped production software at scale. He had led engineering teams. He had done it all.

But alongside his career, he had been quietly building Moxy Messenger — an iOS app that lets people schedule text messages, emails, and WhatsApp messages to send at the perfect moment. And in 2024, Moxy gave him something his salary never could: the freedom to step away.

He spent a year living in Thailand. Exploring the country. Reflecting on a decade of building. And returning with sharper instincts, a broader worldview, and a clarity about what it means to build software that truly sets you free.

The Moxy Philosophy
Say the right thing at the right time — even when life has you on the other side of the world.

🇹🇭 2024 — Thailand
What a year of freedom taught him about building software that matters

Most people build apps hoping to one day have freedom. Terence built Moxy and got on a plane. That year in Thailand clarified what the next decade of his career will look like — and who he wants to build it with.

Moxy Messenger

An iOS message-scheduling platform for text, email, WhatsApp, Slack, and more. 15,000+ users, 4.7★ rating, and the product that funded a year of living freely in Southeast Asia. A communications tool with a clear business user segment and untapped B2B potential.

For Writers & Filmmakers

This story
writes itself.

Every element of a great documentary or narrative short is already here. A small-town origin. A spark moment. A presidential nod. A corporate peak. An escape to Southeast Asia. A return with purpose. If you're a filmmaker, journalist, or writer looking for a story worth telling — this is it.

Act I — Origin
Oakland, MS. Population under 600.

A first-generation college student from a town most people drive through without stopping. He enrolls at MSU on faith, walks into a freshman seminar, and discovers the thing that will change his life.

Act II — The Climb
12 apps. Apple. The White House.

He publishes app after app, wins a WWDC scholarship that takes him to San Francisco, gets nominated by a sitting president, wins entrepreneur competitions, and then does it again for a decade in corporate America.

Act III — The Freedom
Software Manager. Thailand. What's next.

At the peak of his corporate career, an app he built on the side gives him the freedom to step away. He spends a year in Thailand. He comes back knowing exactly what he wants to build — and for whom.

A short documentary or narrative film proposal

From Mississippi
to the World

The story of Terence Williams — founder, engineer, dreamer

There is a moment in every great origin story when a door opens that was never supposed to open for someone like you. For Terence Williams, that moment happened in a freshman seminar room at Mississippi State University — the fall of 2013, a Tuesday, a class called iProgram taught by a professor named Rodney Pearson.

Terence grew up in Oakland, Mississippi. Fewer than 600 people. The kind of town where ambition is respected but rarely resourced. He had been exposed to engineering through the Upward Bound TRiO Program at his grade school in neighboring Coffeeville — a federal initiative designed precisely for students like him: first-generation, high potential, under resourced. That program planted a seed. MSU gave it sunlight.

By his senior year, Terence had published twelve mobile applications to the Apple App Store. Self-taught. On his own time, while completing a degree in electrical engineering. One of them — Bully Walk, a custom campus GPS he built because he remembered being a lost freshman on a campus that felt enormous, would change his life. It would take him to San Francisco. To the White House. To front pages.

In 2016, Apple selected him as one of 350 WWDC scholars worldwide. When they called to offer the scholarship, Terence told them he wouldn't go without someone he loved. Apple covered the extra flight. That small detail tells you everything about who Terence Williams is.

Then came the call from the Obama White House. A Local Frontier nomination recognizing technology entrepreneurs driving impact in underserved communities. A kid from Oakland, Mississippi, standing in front of a stage that read “The White House Frontiers Conference.” He took a photo. The backdrop says everything the words cannot.

What followed was a decade of professional engineering. He climbed. He shipped. He led. He became a Software Engineering Manager at a company most people would be thrilled to work for. And on the side, quietly, he kept building an app called Moxy Messenger — a simple idea: schedule your messages to send at the right moment.

In 2024, Moxy gave him something his salary never could. He stepped away from corporate life and spent a year in Thailand. Not retired. Not lost. Thinking. Exploring. Building. Returning.

The story of Terence Williams is not a story about apps. It is a story about what happens when someone from a place that wasn't supposed to produce them decides to build anyway. It is a story about a boy who got lost on a college campus and turned that confusion into software that has since guided over a million students. It is a story about freedom — the kind you build, not the kind you inherit.

From Mississippi to the world. The question now is: where does someone like Terence Williams go from here?

Key Story Elements
Small-Town Origin
Oakland, MS. Population under 600. First-generation tech entrepreneur.
The Mentor
Professor Rodney Pearson — the iProgram class that changed everything.
The Turning Point
Apple flies his wife to San Francisco because he won't go without her.
The Freedom Chapter
An app funds a year in Thailand. Corporate peak to total freedom.

🎓 Dreamer's Scholarship Fund
$1,000 post-graduate award for Coffeeville High School graduates, presented by Oakland Software Co.
Giving Back

He came from
Coffeeville.
He returned for it.

The kid who left Coffeeville, MS to chase a dream didn't forget where he came from. In 2022, Terence launched the Dreamer's Scholarship Fund through Oakland Software Co. — awarding $1,000 to two graduating seniors from his hometown high school, co-sponsored by the TRiO Upward Bound program that first gave him access to engineering.

He has returned to Coffeeville to speak at the school district, visited Jackson State and other HBCUs to host entrepreneurship workshops, and continues to show young people from small towns that the tech industry has room for them too.

“Great people come from small towns. Coffeeville produced me — I just had to prove it to the world.”
